seo.title: Healthcare Customer Support AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for customer support AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents in healthcare customer support carries life-critical stakes: a misconfigured RAG pipeline or unfiltered PHI leak can trigger HIPAA violations up to $1.5M per incident, expose your organization to malpractice liability, and most importantly, harm patients who rely on accurate information. This checklist operationalizes HIPAA, HITECH, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and SOC 2 Type II requirements into concrete deployment gates for a CRM-integrated, tool-calling RAG agent. Every item maps directly to a regulatory obligation or a patient safety control that must be verified before go-live.

sections.heading: PHI Handling & Data Sovereignty
sections.description: Ensure all patient health information is contained, de-identified before LLM processing, and never transmitted outside the approved processing environment — a non-negotiable HIPAA and HITECH requirement.

items.label: PHI De-identification Pipeline Before LLM Ingestion
items.description: Implement an automated de-identification layer (using NER models or regex rules per HIPAA Safe Harbor or Expert Determination method) that strips all 18 PHI identifiers from CRM records and knowledge base chunks before they are passed to the LLM. Log every de-identification event with a timestamp and record ID.

items.label: Data Residency Enforcement for All RAG Components
items.description: Confirm that the vector database, LLM inference endpoint, CRM integration, and ticket creation tool all operate within a single approved processing environment (e.g., a HIPAA-eligible AWS GovCloud or Azure Government region). Document the data flow diagram and obtain signed BAAs from every vendor in the chain.

items.label: Signed Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) for All Vendors
items.description: Obtain and store executed BAAs from the LLM provider, vector DB vendor, CRM platform, and any third-party tool-calling APIs before processing any real patient data. Verify BAA scope explicitly covers AI/ML processing workloads.

items.label: Outbound Data Egress Blocking at Network Layer
items.description: Configure network policies (VPC security groups, firewall rules) to block all outbound data transfers from the AI processing environment to unapproved external endpoints. Run quarterly egress audits and alert on any anomalous outbound connections.

items.label: Encryption of PHI at Rest and In Transit
items.description: Enforce AES-256 encryption for all stored PHI in the CRM, vector database, and audit logs. Enforce TLS 1.2+ for all data in transit between agent components. Document encryption key management procedures and rotation schedules.

items.label: Minimum Necessary Access Controls on CRM Tool Calls
items.description: Restrict the AI agent's CRM integration credentials to read-only access for order lookup and to scoped write access for ticket creation only. Apply role-based access control (RBAC) so the agent cannot access billing records, clinical notes, or fields outside its defined support scope.

items.label: PHI Retention and Deletion Policy for Agent Logs
items.description: Define a maximum retention period for any agent conversation logs that may contain PHI (default: 6 years per HIPAA, unless state law requires longer). Implement automated deletion workflows and verify they execute on schedule with deletion receipts.

sections.heading: Auditability & FDA 21 CFR Part 11 Compliance
sections.description: Every AI-assisted decision, tool call, and knowledge base retrieval must produce a tamper-evident audit trail that satisfies FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic records requirements and enables post-incident forensic review.

items.label: Immutable Audit Log for Every Agent Action
items.description: Log every agent action — including RAG retrieval queries, retrieved document IDs, LLM prompt/response pairs (with PHI stripped), CRM tool calls, ticket IDs created, and escalation events — to an append-only, tamper-evident log store (e.g., AWS CloudTrail, Azure Immutable Blob Storage). Each log entry must include a timestamp, session ID, user ID, and action type.

items.label: Electronic Signature Linkage for Escalated Decisions
items.description: When the agent escalates a case to a human agent or clinician, capture the receiving agent's authenticated identity (per 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature requirements) and link it to the originating AI session log. This creates an unbroken chain of custody for any decision that affects patient care.

items.label: Audit Log Integrity Verification (Hash Chaining)
items.description: Implement cryptographic hash chaining or digital signatures on audit log entries so any tampering is detectable. Run automated integrity checks daily and alert the compliance team on any hash mismatch.

items.label: Retrieval Source Attribution in Agent Responses
items.description: Configure the RAG agent to include source document IDs and knowledge base version numbers in its internal reasoning trace for every response. This allows auditors to verify which policy document or clinical guideline informed a given answer at a specific point in time.

items.label: Knowledge Base Version Control and Change Audit
items.description: Maintain a versioned history of all knowledge base documents with author, approval date, and change description. When a document is updated, log the version transition and ensure the agent's retrieval index reflects only approved, current versions. Align with 21 CFR Part 11 document control requirements.

items.label: Periodic Audit Log Review and Anomaly Reporting
items.description: Schedule monthly compliance reviews of agent audit logs to identify unusual patterns (e.g., high PHI retrieval volume, repeated escalation failures, off-hours tool calls). Produce a signed compliance report and retain it for a minimum of 6 years.

items.label: Audit Trail Accessibility for Regulatory Inspections
items.description: Ensure audit logs are exportable in a human-readable, structured format (e.g., JSON or CSV) within 24 hours of a regulatory request. Test this export process quarterly and document the procedure in your incident response plan.

sections.heading: Response Accuracy, PII Filtering & Escalation Controls
sections.description: The agent must deliver clinically accurate, policy-compliant responses, filter all PII from outputs, and reliably trigger human escalation for high-risk scenarios — failures here directly translate to patient harm and malpractice exposure.

items.label: Automated PII/PHI Output Filter on Every Agent Response
items.description: Deploy a post-generation PII scanning layer (e.g., AWS Comprehend Medical, Microsoft Presidio, or a custom NER model) that inspects every agent response before delivery to the user. If PHI is detected in the output, block the response, log the event, and substitute a safe fallback message. Test with synthetic PHI datasets monthly.

items.label: Mandatory Escalation Triggers for Clinical or Safety Queries
items.description: Define and hard-code a list of escalation trigger phrases and intent categories (e.g., medication dosage questions, adverse event reports, suicidal ideation, requests for clinical advice) that immediately route the conversation to a licensed human agent. These triggers must not be overridable by the LLM's own reasoning.

items.label: Response Accuracy Validation Against Approved Knowledge Base
items.description: Before go-live, run a minimum of 200 labeled test cases covering common support scenarios (insurance questions, appointment scheduling, medication refill status, billing disputes). Require ≥95% factual accuracy against approved policy documents. Re-run this evaluation after every knowledge base update.

items.label: Hallucination Detection and Confidence Thresholding
items.description: Implement a retrieval confidence score threshold: if the RAG agent cannot retrieve a document with similarity score above the defined threshold (e.g., 0.75 cosine similarity), the agent must respond with a safe fallback ('I don't have that information — let me connect you with a specialist') rather than generating an unsupported answer.

items.label: Tone and Empathy Consistency Evaluation
items.description: Establish a tone rubric aligned with your patient communication policy (e.g., empathetic, non-alarmist, non-diagnostic). Run automated tone scoring (using a fine-tuned classifier or LLM-as-judge) on a random 10% sample of live conversations weekly. Flag responses scoring below threshold for human review.

items.label: Policy Compliance Guardrails for Prohibited Response Types
items.description: Implement hard guardrails that prevent the agent from providing specific diagnoses, recommending prescription changes, quoting specific legal liability positions, or making promises about insurance coverage outcomes. Test these guardrails with adversarial prompts quarterly.

items.label: Escalation Handoff Quality Assurance
items.description: When escalating to a human agent, the AI must pass a structured handoff summary including: session ID, patient-reported issue category, relevant CRM order/ticket IDs, and a flag if any escalation trigger was activated. Audit 100% of escalation handoffs for completeness in the first 30 days post-launch.

items.label: Dead-End and Loop Detection in Conversation Flows
items.description: Implement a session monitor that detects if a patient has repeated the same query more than 3 times without resolution or has been in the same conversation state for more than 5 minutes. Automatically escalate these sessions to a human agent to prevent patient frustration and potential care delays.

sections.heading: Access Control, Incident Response & SOC 2 Type II Readiness
sections.description: Operational security controls, breach response procedures, and continuous monitoring are required to maintain SOC 2 Type II certification and meet HIPAA breach notification obligations under the HITECH Act.

items.label: Multi-Factor Authentication for All Agent Administration Access
items.description: Require MFA for all personnel accessing the agent configuration console, knowledge base management interface, CRM integration credentials, and audit log systems. Enforce this via your identity provider (e.g., Okta, Azure AD) and audit access logs monthly for unauthorized attempts.

items.label: HIPAA Breach Notification Procedure for AI-Related Incidents
items.description: Document a specific incident response runbook for AI agent PHI exposure events. The runbook must include: immediate containment steps (disable agent endpoint), forensic log preservation, breach risk assessment (per HHS 4-factor test), and notification timelines (60 days to HHS, 60 days to affected individuals per HITECH). Test this runbook in a tabletop exercise annually.

items.label: Continuous Security Monitoring and Anomaly Alerting
items.description: Deploy SIEM integration (e.g., Splunk, Datadog, AWS Security Hub) to monitor agent infrastructure for anomalous access patterns, unexpected data volume spikes, failed authentication attempts, and unusual tool-call frequencies. Configure alerts with <15-minute response SLAs for critical severity events.

items.label: Penetration Testing of Agent API Endpoints and CRM Integration
items.description: Conduct a third-party penetration test of all agent-facing APIs, the CRM integration layer, and the ticket creation tool before go-live and annually thereafter. Specifically test for prompt injection attacks, CRM credential exposure, and unauthorized PHI retrieval via the RAG pipeline. Remediate all critical and high findings before production deployment.

items.label: Role-Based Access Control Audit for Support Staff
items.description: Quarterly, review and recertify all human support agent roles that have access to AI-generated summaries, escalation queues, and CRM records. Remove access for terminated employees within 24 hours. Document this process as a SOC 2 Type II access review control.

items.label: Vendor Risk Assessment for All Third-Party Integrations
items.description: Before integrating any new tool (e.g., a new CRM plugin, a third-party knowledge base provider), complete a formal vendor risk assessment that includes HIPAA compliance verification, SOC 2 Type II report review, and data processing agreement execution. Maintain a vendor risk register updated annually.

items.label: Disaster Recovery and Agent Failover Testing
items.description: Define RTO and RPO targets for the AI agent system (recommended: RTO <4 hours, RPO <1 hour for a critical healthcare support channel). Test failover to a human-only support queue at least twice per year and document results. Ensure patients are never left without a support pathway during agent downtime.

items.label: Annual HIPAA Security Risk Analysis Covering AI Systems
items.description: Include the AI agent infrastructure, RAG pipeline, and CRM integration explicitly in your annual HIPAA Security Rule risk analysis (45 CFR § 164.308(a)(1)). Document identified risks, likelihood/impact ratings, and mitigation measures. Retain the risk analysis for 6 years.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: HIPAA Privacy Rule (45 CFR § 164.514) requires that PHI be de-identified using either the Safe Harbor method (removing all 18 specified identifiers) or the Expert Determination method (statistical verification) before it can be processed by an LLM that is not covered under a BAA. Your de-identification pipeline must be validated and documented — not assumed — before any patient data enters the RAG retrieval chain.

FDA 21 CFR Part 11 applies when your AI agent creates, modifies, or retrieves electronic records that are used to support regulated activities (e.g., adverse event reporting, prescription refill requests, or clinical trial support queries). All such records must have audit trails, be protected from unauthorized alteration, and — where signatures are required — use compliant electronic signature mechanisms linked to a specific individual.

The HITECH Act (42 U.S.C. § 17931) extends HIPAA breach notification obligations to Business Associates and increases penalty tiers based on culpability: penalties range from $100 per violation (unknowing) to $50,000 per violation (willful neglect not corrected), with an annual cap of $1.5M per violation category. An AI agent that inadvertently exposes PHI in a response constitutes a reportable breach if the PHI was not encrypted or otherwise rendered unusable — making output-layer PHI filtering a direct financial control, not just a best practice.

SOC 2 Type II certification requires that your security controls operate effectively over a minimum 6-month observation period, not just at a point in time. For AI agent deployments, this means your access controls, audit logging, incident response procedures, and vendor management processes must be continuously operational and evidenced — not assembled retroactively before an audit. Engage your auditor early to map AI-specific controls (e.g., prompt injection testing, RAG retrieval logging) to the Trust Services Criteria.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your healthcare AI agent against this checklist — automatically flagging PHI leaks, audit gaps, and escalation failures before they become HIPAA violations.
